coredata.dc:title: A solution framework for the experimental data shortage problem of lithium-ion batteries: Generative adversarial network-based data augmentation for battery state estimation

coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Jinghua
Gu, Ankun
Kainz, Josef
coredata.dc:description: In order to address the widespread data shortage problem in battery research, this paper proposes a generative adversarial network model that combines it with deep convolutional networks, the Wasserstein distance, and the gradient penalty to achieve data augmentation. To lower the threshold for implementing the proposed method, transfer learning is further introduced. The W-DC-GAN-GP-TL framework is thereby formed. This framework is evaluated on 3 different publicly available datasets to judge the quality of generated data. Through visual comparisons and the examination of two visualization methods (probability density function (PDF) and principal component analysis (PCA)), it is demonstrated that the generated data is hard to distinguish from the real data. The application of generated data for training a battery state model using transfer learning is further evaluated. Specifically, Bi-GRU-based and Transformer-based methods are implemented on 2 separate datasets for estimating state of health (SOH) and state of charge (SOC), respectively. The results indicate that the proposed framework demonstrates satisfactory performance in different scenarios: for the data replacement scenario, where real data are removed and replaced with generated data, the state estimator accuracy decreases only slightly; for the data enhancement scenario, the estimator accuracy is further improved. The estimation accuracy of SOH and SOC is as low as 0.69% and 0.58% root mean square error (RMSE) after applying the proposed framework. This framework provides a reliable method for enriching battery measurement data. It is a generalized framework capable of generating a variety of time series data.

head.ce:title: A solution framework for the experimental data shortage problem of lithium-ion batteries: Generative adversarial network-based data augmentation for battery state estimation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work proposes a framework for generating experimental data based on generative adversarial networks. While applying transfer learning to lower the threshold of use, this framework can significantly improve the efficiency of battery state estimators.

In order to address the widespread data shortage problem in battery research, this paper proposes a generative adversarial network model that combines it with deep convolutional networks, the Wasserstein distance, and the gradient penalty to achieve data augmentation. To lower the threshold for implementing the proposed method, transfer learning is further introduced. The W-DC-GAN-GP-TL framework is thereby formed. This framework is evaluated on 3 different publicly available datasets to judge the quality of generated data. Through visual comparisons and the examination of two visualization methods (probability density function (PDF) and principal component analysis (PCA)), it is demonstrated that the generated data is hard to distinguish from the real data. The application of generated data for training a battery state model using transfer learning is further evaluated. Specifically, Bi-GRU-based and Transformer-based methods are implemented on 2 separate datasets for estimating state of health (SOH) and state of charge (SOC), respectively. The results indicate that the proposed framework demonstrates satisfactory performance in different scenarios: for the data replacement scenario, where real data are removed and replaced with generated data, the state estimator accuracy decreases only slightly; for the data enhancement scenario, the estimator accuracy is further improved. The estimation accuracy of SOH and SOC is as low as 0.69% and 0.58% root mean square error (RMSE) after applying the proposed framework. This framework provides a reliable method for enriching battery measurement data. It is a generalized framework capable of generating a variety of time series data.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their superior energy density, extended service life, and additional benefits, lithium-ion batteries find extensive application in energy storage systems, electric vehicles, etc. However, the inconsistency of cells within a module and the gradual decline of battery performance throughout its lifespan have negatively impacted the effective operation of those energy storage systems [1]. The reliable operation of lithium-ion batteries in complex environments depends on a highly efficient battery management system (BMS). Timely and accurately monitoring the battery state is a prerequisite for BMS to function optimally [2].

Battery states that are critical for stable operation include state of charge (SOC), state of health (SOH), state of energy (SOE), state of power (SOP), etc. [3]. Two of the most influential metrics for BMS are SOH and SOC [4,5]. Accurate SOH estimation provides a comprehensive understanding of the battery status throughout its lifespan. This allows for optimization of battery management strategies according to unique battery characteristics in varying states [6]. Additionally, it enables the further development of appropriate maintenance and replacement schedules [7]. The SOC provides information about the available capacity of the battery. This information assists in optimizing the execution of charging strategies and avoiding over-charging/discharging or cell imbalance [8].

Nevertheless, because nonlinear electrochemical phenomena in the battery are significantly influenced by operating conditions, they have inevitably been a challenge for BMS when estimating the battery state. Generally speaking, the mainstream research methods for the estimation of SOH or SOC fall into two categories, namely model-based and data-driven methods [9,10]. The most commonly employed model-based approach involves an equivalent circuit model (ECM). Although this model cannot completely describe the complex electrochemical processes inside the battery [11], it has modest demands in terms of computational resources. This method is capable of capturing both battery dynamic and steady-state characteristics. For example, Huang et al. [12] proposed an improved adaptive ECM with which SOH estimation can be extended to a range of different battery chemistries. ECM-based SOC estimation is typically combined with additional filtering algorithms, which minimize the error between estimates and observations. Validated combinations include ECM with Kalman filter [13], ECM with particle filter [14], and others.

However, the performance of model-based approaches is critically dependent on the precision and robustness of established battery models, especially since the variation of model parameters during aging and charging/discharging is a nonlinear process [15]. This implies that the resource investment required to obtain a robust model, which describes the battery accurately under various operating conditions, is significant. Therefore, data-driven methods are deemed advantageous since they do not require a priori knowledge. They are capable of dealing with any nonlinear mapping relationship. Thus, SOH/SOC estimation is achieved by establishing a mapping relationship between input characteristics and the actual battery state. Sun et al. [16] proposed an approach employing current interrupt measurements to obtain equivalent impedance values significantly correlated with the SOH. In addition, an optimized feedforward network for accurate SOH estimation was introduced. Zhao et al. [17] have efficiently utilized the ability of convolutional neural network (CNNs) to extract features and recurrent neural network (RNNs) to capture long-term dependencies to develop an integrated CNN-RNN network for SOC estimation. Overall, numerous data-driven techniques such as feedforward neural network (FFNN), recurrent neural network (RNN), and Bayesian network (BN) have been successfully applied for battery state estimation with satisfactory results [18].

Battery state estimation can be sufficiently accurate using data-driven methods. However, the accompanying challenges are considerable: data-driven methods require large amounts of historical data to train the model. The accuracy of the method depends primarily on the quantity and quality of the available training data. In practice, due to the fluctuating nature of operating conditions, the data collected is typically characterized by strong noise interference. Therefore, the data quality is frequently unsatisfactory [19]. Data collection in the laboratory environment is strictly limited by test capacity. This is an extremely resource-intensive and time-consuming task that burdens small research groups. In addition, experimental failures due to personnel/instrument mishandling during the aging cycle often cause loss of experimental data, which typically cannot be compensated because the calendar and cycle aging of the experimental cell are continuously progressing which makes the repetition of failed measurements impossible. Therefore, such a loss is almost impossible to make good, especially for work that relies solely on its own limited-size datasets. Furthermore, the number of publicly available datasets is severely constrained, e.g., because companies want to protect their know-how. The lack of available experimental data due to this set of reasons seriously impacts the training of machine learning models. This inevitably hinders technology advancement in this area.

Data augmentation techniques are a non-resource-intensive approach to counter experimental data shortage [20]. The proposed data augmentation techniques can be divided into general data augmentation and generative modeling methods. General time-series data augmentation techniques employ mathematical or probabilistic models to process and generate data. These methods include interpolation, trend superposition, window clipping, weighted dynamic time warping barycentric averaging (WDTWA), etc. [21,22]. In the domain of battery technology, Zhang et al. [23] demonstrated a method for enhancing data by integrating Gaussian noise into the voltage, current, and temperature data of lithium-ion batteries. This technique significantly improved the predictive accuracy of SOC and state of energy (SOE) across diverse operating conditions. Kim et al. [24] introduced a data augmentation strategy employing dynamic time warping (DTW) to mitigate the issue of limited run-to-failure (RTF) data in lithium-ion batteries and other engineering systems. Santoni et al. [25] generated expanded datasets through random sampling, utilizing Monte Carlo simulation to fit a probability distribution based on experimental data. In a complementary approach, Han et al. [26] dynamically derived equivalent circuit characteristic parameters of lithium-ion batteries using recursive least squares with forgetting factor (RLSFF) and key-point linear projection (KLP). Furthermore, Gao et al. [27] applied quantum assimilation methods to supplement and enhance test data of anomalous batteries. While these methodologies effectively preserve the intrinsic features of the original datasets and serve well for the initial expansion of small-scale datasets, they exhibit limitations in capturing intricate nonlinear relationships and high-dimensional dependencies, leading to restricted diversity in the generated data. To address these limitations, generative modeling methods have become increasingly prevalent.

Generative modeling methods serve as powerful tools for tasks such as battery performance prediction and degradation modeling by effectively capturing the underlying distributional characteristics of data. The primary methodologies include variational autoencoders (VAEs), Transformer-based generative models, Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), and their extensions. VAEs are distinguished by their stability during training and their capability to encompass global distributional characteristics. However, their reliance on probabilistic inference—specifically, the sampling of the latent space—and the smoothing of this space can lead to generating data that is imprecise and lacks detail, particularly in the context of complex, high-dimensional datasets [28]. Conversely, Transformer-based generative models excel at capturing long-term dependencies and global patterns; however, they require access to large-scale, high-quality datasets to achieve optimal performance [29]. GANs are particularly notable in battery data generation due to their proficiency in producing realistic and diverse data samples. This ability makes them particularly suitable for modeling the complex nonlinear relationships and varied patterns that characterize battery data. This is due to deep generative adversarial networks aim to learn data distribution through adversarial training. The joint training of the generator and discriminator makes possible the generation of samples that are indistinguishable from real samples. Although the idea of utilizing time-series GANs to generate battery experimental data has advanced considerably [30], barriers to widespread GAN application in this field continue to exist. First of all, electrochemical processes inside the battery are decisively influenced by operating conditions. Experimental data under various conditions exhibit extremely diverse characteristics. However, GANs cannot generate data under specified conditions. This shortcoming can be compensated by the application of conditional generative adversarial networks (CGAN) [31]. For example, Liu et al. [32] utilized a CGAN to reconstruct missing voltage data under multiple levels of data incompleteness and thus achieved accurate SOH estimation. Secondly, the loss function of traditional GAN cannot indicate training progress. This means that the training stability of the model cannot be guaranteed and the number of necessary iterations is undeterminable. Proposed to address this drawback is WGAN-GP (Wasserstein GAN with gradient penalty) [33], which provides an explicit loss function for GANs and does not suffer from gradient vanishing or gradient explosion. For example, Qiu et al. [34] used an LSTM-WGAN-GP model to generate experimental data for time series and perform accurate SOC estimation.

Similar to battery state estimators, GAN training also requires a certain amount of data. The wide variety of lithium-ion batteries and the different sizes/formats of datasets present challenges for GAN applications. To reduce the burden, transfer learning (TL) is increasingly employed to adapt the original model to different battery datasets. For example, Li et al. [35] introduced deep transfer learning to accelerate the model training process, then developed state estimators for multiple data forms, which enhanced the robustness and generalization of the model. This implies that TL has the potential to be applied to GAN training as well.

In summary, this work aims to utilize GAN to generate pseudo-experimental data suitable for battery state estimation, to address scientific research limitations due to data shortages. In order to accomplish this, GAN needs to overcome several problems. Combining the strengths of CGAN and WGAN-GP, a C-DC-WGAN-GP network (conditional deep convolutional Wasserstein generative adversarial network with gradient penalty) with CNN as generator and discriminator is proposed. Convolutional networks provide significant advantages in training speed and structured feature extraction. They effectively reduce model complexity through a parameter-sharing mechanism, specifically utilizing convolutional kernels. This approach enhances the efficiency of the training process and facilitates easier convergence of the model. Thus, the system should be able to train and generate data with excellent stability under specified conditions. It further prevents gradient explosion or gradient vanishing. In addition, transfer learning is subsequently incorporated into this network and constitutes the framework of C-DC-WGAN-GP-TL. This framework enables the training of GAN by utilizing larger datasets and afterward transferring the model to smaller datasets. This effectively lowers the threshold for GAN application and also solves the problem of data shortage to a significant extent. Moreover, the usefulness of data generated under diverse scenarios for accurate SOH and SOC estimation will be demonstrated: In a first scenario the data generated by GAN serves as data enhancement in the absence of sufficient experimental data, while in a second scenario the data generated by GAN provide data replacement for failures during the aging test.

The remaining sections of this paper are organized as follows: Section 2 lists the methodology employed in this paper, including the technical background and a description of the framework's structure; Section 3 describes the data utilized in this work and the corresponding data preprocessing methods; Section 4 presents analysis results, including an assessment of the quality of the GAN generated data, and a detailed analysis further illustrating the benefits of the proposed framework for estimating battery state; Section 5 draws a conclusion.

Accurate data-driven battery state estimation places considerable demands on both the quality and quantity of measurement data. Lack of measurement capability and mistakes in the measurement process limit data availability. In this work, generative adversarial networks are employed for data generation and augmentation. Adversarial training is utilized to learn the latent distribution of existing measurements with the aim of generating fresh data comparable to real measurements. This is done to alleviate the bottleneck caused by the lack of experimental data in different scenarios.

Generative Adversarial Networks are a class of deep learning frameworks designed to generate “pseudo-real data” [20]. This unsupervised generative model is composed of two neural networks trained alternately: a generator (G) and a discriminator (D). In essence, the aim of GAN training is to establish a Nash equilibrium between the generator and the discriminator, which is achieved through adversarial training. Thus, the generator captures the real data distribution in order to generate data similar to it to deceive the discriminator; meanwhile, the discriminator attempts to differentiate the real data from the generated data as much as possible.

The data generated by GAN is generally random and thereby qualifies as unsupervised learning. Therefore, in order to generate measurement data under specified conditions, supervised learning techniques are incorporated into GANs [31]. Conditional GAN (CGAN) is such a concept. Its structure is represented by Fig. 1 . It provides a highly targeted approach to network training. During training, the GAN is fed by not only random noise but also additional labeling information. Based on the conventional GAN, a condition variable
c
is incorporated into the inputs of D and G. This enables the generator not just to distinguish whether the input data are from real samples, but also to judge whether the data satisfy the distribution mapping relationship under the corresponding conditions. Therefore, CGAN's objective function is as follows

In the training process of a typical GAN, the generator
G

The gradient descent algorithm is used for both alternating training processes. CGAN's generator not only attempts to render the generated pseudo-samples similar to the real samples, but it also aims to make them match the labels. Accordingly, CGAN's discriminator must determine whether the input samples match the labels in addition to its original task of determining whether input samples are real or generated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wasserstein generative adversarial networks with gradient penalty
ce:section.ce:para: GAN is undoubtedly a groundbreaking technique. However, since its inception, it has suffered from difficulties in training, generator and discriminator losses that cannot indicate the training process, as well as insufficient diversity in the generated samples. These deficiencies can be largely attributed to the loss functions employed in conventional GAN models, namely KL (Kullback-Leibler) divergence or JS (Jensen-Shannon) divergence. The primary problems with conventional GANs can be classified as follows [36].

Firstly, when
p r
(real sample distribution) and
p g
(generated sample distribution) are not overlapping, their JS divergence is invariably a constant (
log 2
). This means the gradient is 0, and thus, the generator cannot be optimized by the gradient descent method. Meanwhile, the phenomenon of non-overlap of
p r
and
p g
may occur throughout the GAN alternating training process for various reasons. This results in the observation that GANs are challenging to train.

Secondly, conventional GANs penalize different types of generator errors differentially. Specifically, for a particular sample
x
, the penalty for the first type of error, i.e., “the generator fails to generate a real sample” (
p r
x
→ 1 &
p g
x
→ 0
), is insignificant, while the penalty for the second type of error, i.e., “the generator generates an unreal sample” (
p r
x
→ 0 &
p g
x
→ 1
), suffers a substantial penalty. Therefore, the generator tends to generate duplicate but safe samples rather than samples with diversity. This problem is referred to as mode collapse.

All the above problems are solvable by the application of novel similarity metrics, namely, the Wasserstein distance [37]. In order to solve it directly during GAN training, a simplification based on Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality [38] is applied

Despite its solid theoretical foundation and relatively straightforward implementation, the problems regarding training difficulty in practical applications are not yet completely eliminated. In fact, the issue lies in that the Lipschitz constraint is not properly enforced here [39]: in practice
f
x

w
. To enforce the Lipschitz constraint on the parameters, a method to restrict parameters
w

[ - c , c ]
is widely employed, which is also recognized as weight clipping. However, weight clipping introduces new problems: e.g., weight clipping may cause the parameters to be basically at the constraints' bounding values (weight binarization), which in turn may cause the gradient to vanish or explode without careful parameter tuning.

To further address the above problem, an enhanced version of WGAN, the Wasserstein generative adversarial network with gradient penalty (WGAN-GP), is proposed [33]. It provides an alternative approach to enforcing the Lipschitz constraint, which is called a gradient penalty. Specifically, WGAN-GP adds a regular term to the loss function of the WGAN discriminator; the improved discriminator loss function is as follows

In contrast to the conventional GAN, which cannot indicate the training process, the enhanced loss function can indicate training progress. Furthermore, it is possible to assess the model's quality utilizing the Wasserstein distance between the real and generated data distributions.

In order to generate “pseudo-real data” under specified conditions, which then can be used to perform data augmentation or data replacement for diverse scenarios for the purpose of improving battery state estimation accuracy, a complete solution framework for the experimental data shortage problem is proposed in this work. This framework is illustrated in Fig. 2 , which can be divided into four parts.
1.
Battery aging experiments from BoL (Begin of life) to EoL (End of life) are performed. Measurement data are sampled at a high sampling rate during the charging process. Following the collection of a complete set of battery charging data, the obtained data are subjected to preliminary processing (including the elimination of anomalous data and outliers).
2.
“Pseudo-real data” is generated by the proposed battery data augmentation model. The proposed model is a deeply developed variant of GAN called a conditional deep convolutional Wasserstein generative adversarial network with gradient penalty (C-DC-WGAN-GP). This model generates information about voltage, current, and capacity during charging under specified conditions. The detailed structure of the generator and discriminator is illustrated in Fig. 2, and a detailed discussion follows in the next subsection. Overall, this GAN variant has considerable advantages over traditional GANs: the deep learning network functions as a generator and discriminator to enhance the model's generalization ability, the application of the conditional adversarial training concept facilitates the model to generate data under specified conditions, and the loss function is improved by the application of the Wasserstein distance as well as gradient penalty to minimize the training difficulty.
3.
Extending data augmentation models' applicability by utilizing transfer learning. Since the training of GAN models still requires a certain amount of data, training a pre-trained model on an easily accessible large dataset and then migrating it to a fresh smaller dataset will substantially reduce the threshold for application of the proposed framework.
4.
Data enhancement or data replacement is performed with generated data in various scenarios. The dataset consisting of both real and generated data will be fed into the state prediction model as time-series data for accurate battery state estimation.

The generator network is trained to learn the distribution of real measurement data, and its architecture is illustrated in Fig. 2. Table 1 lists its specific parameter configurations. The generator network generates an initial feature map of size 512 × 37 by splicing noise vectors and label vectors through a fully connected (FC) layer and reshaped operation. The final samples of size 3 × 300 are then generated by stepwise up-sampling through 3 layers of 1d transposed convolutional layers (ConvTranspose1d) [40]. Where transpose-convolution (or convolution) operations produce non-integer output lengths, this work rounds down the output length. The first and second transposed convolution layers employ an activation function identified as LeakyReLU [41], which retains minor gradients on the negative axis, thereby avoiding gradient vanishing and accelerating model convergence. The third layer then implements the tanh activation function, which normalizes the output range within [−1, 1]. In summary, the input to the generator network is a vector of dimensions (100 + 3) × 1, constructed by concatenating a noise vector of length 100 with labeling information transformed by the embedding layer into a three-digit numerical format. The generator produces time series data with dimensions of 3 × 300.

The discriminator network trains to distinguish between real measurements and generated data sequences. Furthermore, it determines whether the samples match a given label. For this purpose, the discriminator network splices the input samples with the label vectors, and completes the step-by-step feature extraction through 3 layers of 1D convolutional layers (Conv1D). Finally, it outputs the discriminator's assessment of the samples' authenticity through a 1-layer FC layer. Its architecture is also depicted in Fig. 2, while the specific parameter configurations are detailed in Table 2 . The activation function for all convolutional layers is LeakyReLU. In summary, the discriminator input comprises either the generator output or real data of equivalent dimensions, combined with label information to create a dataset with dimensions of (3 + 3) × 300. Specifically, for a particular data sample, the input entails a three-digit number of label information alongside the variables U, I, and Q pertinent to that specific sample point. The output of the discriminator is a score with dimensions of 1 × 1, which indicates the result of the judgment. Moreover, the batch size of both the generator and the discriminator is set to 32. Besides, the performance of the GAN is sensitive to variations in learning rate and batch size. Therefore, we conducted a series of experiments to identify the optimal values for these hyperparameters. A comprehensive description of the method employed can be found in Appendix A.

The generator and discriminator architectures described above apply the design of a classic deep convolutional GAN (DCGAN). Their optimization process is an adversarial iterative process with the intention of overlapping distributions of real and generated data. To ensure fast, accurate, and efficient training of the GAN, preferably several model training techniques are applied to the training of the C-DC-WGAN-GP proposed in this paper.
•
Learning rate decay: the learning rate after each epoch is multiplied by 0.93. This setting ensures that the model converges rapidly in the preliminary stage of training, while in the subsequent stage, the model parameters can be fine-tuned by reducing step sizes to approach the optimal solution more stably and avoid oscillations. Different configurations of learning rate decay have been evaluated and validated. The results indicate that a decay rate of 0.93 can achieve convergence as fast as possible while ensuring stable training.
•
A combination of spectral normalization [42] and batch normalization [43] is employed: the discriminator performs spectral normalization, which helps the discriminator to ensure that the output satisfies the 1-Lipschitz condition, thus efficiently preventing the gradient from exploding or vanishing; the generator implements batch normalization, which ensures that the outputs of each batch have similar mean and variance, thus maintaining the smoothness of the generator's gradient and the stability of the training process.
•
Discriminator and generator training frequencies are different: the discriminator training is executed 5 times before each generator update. This strategy can effectively prevent the generator from updating too rapidly in the early stages, thus avoiding the learning imbalance between the generator and the discriminator. While the discriminator can learn and converge more quickly under this strategy, then provide more accurate gradient information to the generator. This will enable the generator to improve the quality of generated samples based on stable feedback.
•
Customized weight initialization: the appropriate initialization strategy is identified according to the layer types (convolutional layer, transposed convolutional layer, fully connected layer, etc.) and corresponding activation functions. For layers with tanh activation function, Xavier initialization [44] helps to balance the variance of the input and output to prevent the gradient from vanishing; for layers with LeakyReLU activation function, Kaiming initialization [45] works well for asymmetric activation functions and keeps the gradient stable. Correct weight initialization guarantees that the model avoids gradient explosion or vanishing at the beginning of training and accelerates the model's convergence process.
•
Maintaining the sample number per label constant: during data pre-processing, the sample number per labeled category remains roughly constant so that the generator can fairly learn the sample distribution under different labels. This balanced data distribution can effectively prevent the generator from generating category bias during the training process, i.e., from generating superior samples for certain categories and inferior quality samples for others.

In principle, GAN training still requires a certain amount of measurement data for support. This raises the threshold for GAN applications considerably, thus reducing the contribution GAN can make to the solution of the data shortage problem. Therefore, it is helpful if the model can be first trained on a large dataset to gain a deeper understanding of the features, then adapted to a more limited dataset through transfer learning [46]. In this way, the model can combine the previously learned features (during training with the large dataset) with information from the limited dataset to generate new valuable measurement data. This reduces the amount of measurement data for transfer learning required in real-world applications, thus lowering the threshold for implementing GANs with the prospect of providing more reliable support in battery state prediction.

During the transfer learning process in this work, since the small dataset is tested at 25/40 °C (there are explanations about the experimental data described in more detail in the next chapter), these data are labeled since they are the closest in condition to the original model (45 °C), then fed into the model for continued training. The model structure remains unchanged, while hyperparameters such as learning rate, training iterations, and batch size are adjusted to match the upcoming training task. Specifically, during the transfer learning process, the following adjustments facilitate the adaptation of the model to its new task.
•
Decrease the learning rate: prevent drastic variations in model parameters and protect crucial features learned.
•
Increase training iterations: allow the model adequate time to fine-tune new tasks, improving model generalization ability on fresh datasets.
•
Reduce the batch size: small batches combined with modest learning rates can improve the model's generalization performance and enhance the diversity of generated samples. However, it may also prolong training times.
•
Eliminate the learning rate controller: by employing a fixed and limited learning rate, the model can maintain stability during fine-tuning and fine-tuning more efficiently.

These adjustments assist the model in adapting efficiently to the new dataset while retaining crucial features learned in the larger dataset. This provides high-quality generated data for subsequent prediction models and further improves battery state prediction accuracy.

The battery state estimator is the last part of the framework proposed in this work. Its structure is also depicted in Fig. 2. In order to discuss more broadly the impact of the proposed data enhancement techniques on state estimation accuracy; two different estimator structures are employed to provide a metric of accuracy: Transformer-based [47] and bidirectional GRU-based (gated recurrent unit) [48] approaches, respectively. The structures of the SOH estimator and the SOC estimator incorporating these two approaches will be discussed in the following subsections.

To handle time series data of different lengths, the transformer-based approach first makes the samples in each batch equal in length by padding. Masking is applied to zero out padding portions during forward propagation and turn off backpropagation. This ensures that the model focuses exclusively on valid sequence data. During training, the 1-dimensional time series data during charging used as input is initially mapped to 32 dimensions by a fully connected layer. Positional information in the form of sines and cosines is added to capture the temporal order in the sequence. The core of this model is a 2-layer transformer encoder, each layer containing 4 multi-head self-attention parts and a feed-forward neural network, which is combined with layer normalization to improve model stability. Lastly, SOH predictions are output through fully connected layers. The model employs the mean square error (MSE) as the loss function and is trained with the Adam optimizer (learning rate of 0.001). The batch size is 4, and training lasts for 100 epochs. Dropout is set to 20% in the model to minimize overfitting. Small batch training can introduce randomness, which is similar to the regularization effect. This helps to improve the generalization ability of the model, thereby helping the model to minimize the influence of noise. Overall, input data sampling is conducted based on the longest sample within the dataset, given that the total lengths of the samples vary. From this longer sample, 300 uniformly distributed sampling points are identified. The voltage data associated with the charging process at these sampling points will be utilized to create the input for the SOH estimator. As a result, the length of data available for input to the SOH estimator differs across samples. The critical parameters of the SOH estimator are outlined in Table 3 .

The Bi-GRU approach applies the same method to processing data of different lengths as the Transformer approach described above. The input is 1-dimensional charging time series data, which is processed by the bidirectional GRU to output a single SOH prediction. With regard to structure and hyperparameters, the model adopts a bidirectional GRU structure with 32 hidden units per layer. The output dimension of the bidirectional GRU doubles that of the unidirectional one, which combines forward and backward hidden states in 2 layers. The batch size is 4, training epochs are 100, and the dropout probability is 20% to prevent overfitting. The loss function is again MSE. The optimizer employs Adam with a 0.001 learning rate. Since in SOH prediction, the training data sequence is fairly lengthy for Bi-GRU, layer normalization is also performed after each GRU layer to enhance model stability and prevent gradient vanishing.

For SOC prediction, the transformer approach needs to initially segment the time series data using the sliding window (window size is 50 in this work) technique. In each slide of the window, the sequence in the window is utilized as input data. The SOC corresponding to the last window step is assigned as a label. For instance, when sampling from the first sample point to the fiftieth, these 50 consecutive voltage values are utilized as inputs for the model. The SOC value associated with the fiftieth sample point serves as the reference or “true value” of the SOC. The generated input sequences and corresponding SOC labels are normalized and employed for model training. This approach relies on a transformer-based encoder structure. The 1-dimensional charging time series data used as input is first mapped to 64 dimensions through a fully connected layer. Position information is added to capture the temporal order in the sequence, followed by three layers of the transformer encoder, each containing four multi-head self-attention parts and feed-forward neural networks. The encoder output passes through the dropout layer (with a dropout rate of 20%) and finally through the fully connected layer to output a SOC prediction. The model utilizes MSE as the loss function and the Adam optimizer (learning rate of 0.001) for training. The batch size is 16, and model training lasts for 50 epochs. The critical parameters of the SOH estimator are outlined in Table 4 .

The Bi-GRU approach also requires implementing the identical sliding window technique as the Transformer approach to preprocessing time series data. The model has 2 layers of GRUs, each containing 64 hidden units. To avoid overfitting, the model incorporates a 20% dropout. The output of the GRU is passed through a fully-connected layer to obtain a SOC prediction. The model is also trained to utilize MSE as the loss function and an Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.001. The training is performed for a total of 50 epochs.

This work involves constructing a GAN model based on a large dataset and then applying it to a small dataset via a transfer learning technique. Subsequently, the effectiveness of the data augmentation model in contributing to the state estimator is verified on a small dataset for various application scenarios. This transfer learning step significantly lowers the threshold for data augmentation techniques. However, it places high demands on the diversity of data sources required for GAN model training. For this reason, this work makes extensive utilization of readily available open-source datasets for model development and training. A brief description of them and the corresponding data preprocessing procedure will appear below.

The dataset provided by Zhu et al. [49] employs a total of three different electrochemical types of batteries for aging cycle experiments. NCA and NMC battery datasets, consisting of 66 cells and 55 cells, respectively, are analyzed in this work. The data required for model training will be selected from within this range. Several cyclic aging conditions are applied to different cells, evaluated at 25, 35, and 45 °C. The currents available for charging and discharging range from 0.25 to 1 C. The measurement conditions for a given cell are constant throughout its entire lifespan. More specific information can be found in the original publication.

The Oxford dataset [50] is compiled by the University of Oxford. A total of 8 lithium-ion batteries with a rated capacity of 740 mAh are investigated. The experiments are conducted at a constant 40 °C. During cycling, the cells are charged at 2 C constant current and discharged under dynamic conditions simulating driving profiles. After every 100 cycles, a 1 C charge/discharge cycle is performed to determine the cell's current capacity. More detailed information is available in the original publication.

For the preprocessing of measurement data, a sequence data processing method is applied in this work. First, raw sequence data of voltage V, current I, charge Q, and other corresponding data during battery charging are obtained. The reason for choosing purely charging data is that this process is a relatively controllable part of a battery's lifespan. By utilizing it exclusively for modeling, it improves the method's applicability. Since the measurement data are collected at fixed time intervals during the charging process, the data size may vary significantly depending on the charging rate and cycle count for each cell. To address this issue, the individual cycle length of the battery with the longest measurement time in the dataset will be determined. As a result, a uniformly distributed set of 300 time points can be identified based on this maximum length. The voltages, currents, and charges corresponding to these 300 time points are calculated by interpolation. The final V, I, and Q vectors for each cell are obtained. The inputs thus processed implicitly represent the battery charging process at a given time. The implementation of data preprocessing methods to create model inputs simplifies the computational process and has the advantage of being applicable to batteries under various charging conditions.

To quantify the advantages of the proposed framework in solving experimental data shortages related to batteries, its performance is examined regarding two aspects. Firstly, the quality of the generated data by W-DC-GAN-GP-TL is comprehensively evaluated from multiple perspectives. These include visual comparison of the generated data with real data to determine whether trends are comparable, as well as quantitative and qualitative assessment. For the latter, principal component analysis (PCA) and probability density function (PDF), which are two commonly employed metrics to determine the similarity between different data distributions, are applied as practical analysis tools to measure the data quality. Secondly, the benefits of the generated data on battery state estimation will also be evaluated in two conceivably typical scenarios.
•
Lack of measurement data: The generated data will be incorporated as data enhancement in the state estimator.
•
Specific data loss due to mishandling during the measurement process: To investigate this, generated data will replace part of measurement data to simulate loss due to measurement failure.
The SOH and SOC estimation accuracy (root mean square error, RMSE) for these two scenarios will be analyzed as a metric for the proposed method's performance.

Overall, the NMC dataset within the Zhu dataset (hereafter called the NMC dataset) will be considered for baseline GAN model training due to its sufficiently large size. The NCA dataset within the Zhu dataset (hereafter called the NCA dataset) and the Oxford dataset will be applied to examine method effectiveness after transfer learning. The estimation of the SOH and the SOC will also apply these two datasets and generated data after transfer learning. The training and test set data required for the battery state estimator come from different cells in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generate data based on the NMC dataset
ce:section.ce:para: The NMC dataset is employed due to its sufficient data amount in training a baseline model intended for subsequent transfer learning. Considering that the dataset employed applies a wide range of charging/discharging rate levels and the data is collected at different SOH levels, when a particular charging/discharging rate or a specific SOH is chosen as the conditional label for the GAN, it is difficult to gather enough data for training with this label. Therefore, temperature serves as the conditional label for this work. Under this premise, each conditional label can obtain roughly one-third of the data in the NMC dataset, which is sufficient to train an accurate model. However, this also results in the inability of the GAN model, which is obtained by training with this publicly available dataset, to generate target data for a specific SOH. However, if the proposed framework can generate high-quality data at specific temperatures, it is reasonable to infer that it can similarly generate data at particular SOHs when the data amount for individual SOHs is large enough.

Each battery test cycle is treated as a sample under the proposed framework. To ensure that the sample amount for each conditional label is roughly balanced (the imbalance situation will be discussed in the next paragraph), the first 20 cells at 25 °C in the NMC dataset, all the 4 cells at 35 °C, and the first 12 cells at 45 °C are selected to train the GAN model. In this case, the sample ratio of the three conditional labels is roughly 1:1:1 (specifically, 4955:4806:4968). Fig. 3 illustrates the quality of the generated data from the NMC dataset when the sample amount is identical for each label. Fig. 3(a–c) demonstrates the comparison between the generated data (with Q max = 3141.88 mAh) and the measurement data (with Q max = 3141.84 mAh). Visually, although the generated data occasionally have fluctuations similar to measurement noise, the overall trend and key features are not distinct from the real data. Furthermore, with the help of suitable filters, it can be reasonably inferred that the generated data after eliminating the abnormal fluctuations cannot be distinguished from the real data by visual inspection. The identical conclusion holds for the two analysis tools, PDF and PCA, whose results are illustrated in Fig. 3(d, e). In Fig. 3(d), the colors representing the real data and the generated data are semi-transparent to avoid covering each other, so that it can be seen clearly that the generated and real data distributions are almost identical. The results of PCA show similarly that the generated and real data have very similar “clouds” of principal component values. Therefore, the generator successfully captured the distribution of measurement data for these 3 temperature conditions. With about 5000 samples for each conditional label, the proposed method generates “pseudo-measurement data” with exceptional accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of the sample equalization degree
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, the excellent training results in Fig. 3 are achieved utilizing balanced samples (roughly equal sample amounts belonging to each conditional label). This poses a challenge for GAN training under certain conditions, since “the amount of water a barrel can hold is determined by the shortest stave.”: If samples have to be balanced and for one label (the shortest stave) only few measurements are available, the number of measurements for all other labels would have to be reduced, which probably would negatively impact model quality. To analyze whether such a limitation can be ignored, the method performance in the case of sample imbalance should be discussed as well. In addition to the balanced sample selection method proposed in the previous subsection, two unbalanced sample ratios are introduced in this work. Specifically, we compare two cases here, which both include all 23 cells at 25 °C and all 4 cells at 35 °C from the NMC dataset. Case 1 additionally includes all 28 cells at 45 °C, whereas Case 2 only contains the first 18 cells for that temperature. The ratios of sample size under the three conditional labels are 5531:4806:17666 for Case 1 and 5531:4806:8896 for Case 2. Fig. 4 shows the discriminator loss for the 3 scenarios mentioned. For WassersteinGAN, the indicator of successful training is the discriminator loss convergence to 0. From the figure, it appears that merely in balanced samples, discriminator loss accomplishes successful convergence. In contrast, the GAN fails to deliver effective training in either unbalanced Case 1 or Case 2. At the maximum imbalance (Case 1), the loss could not even exhibit a trend towards 0. Thus, training is terminated early. In a less imbalanced situation (Case 2), the GAN achieves a slightly better performance than expected: although it does not completely fail to converge as in Case 1, the loss cannot converge completely to 0 after more training iterations than the balanced case. Such a result indicates that the balance of samples is extremely crucial for the proposed framework. It is certain that the one among the various conditional labels with the smallest sample count determines the quality of the GAN training under such a premise.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Training of models after transfer learning
ce:section.ce:para: Although the proposed framework obtains excellent results on the NMC dataset, the nearly 15,000 samples required to train the baseline model are still a number representing considerable measurement effort. It would therefore be highly desirable to extend the applicability of the proposed method by generalization through transfer learning. Therefore, 518 samples (test cycles) from the first 3 cells in the NCA dataset and 366 samples from the Oxford dataset are selected for two separate transfer learning investigations, respectively. The quality of the generated data after transfer learning reflects the generalization ability of the proposed framework. A superior quality of data will guarantee the idea of “training the baseline model on a large dataset and then applying it to a small dataset through transfer learning”.

Similar to the idea of demonstrating the quality of generated data in Fig. 3, Figs. 5 and 6
illustrate the quality of the Oxford generated data and the NCA generated data after transfer learning, respectively. Since the NMC dataset and the Oxford dataset are not correlated in any sense, the baseline model is trained on the Oxford dataset for more (10,000 vs. 2000 for NCA dataset) epochs after transfer. The results presented in Fig. 5 even outperforms the baseline model: noise-like fluctuations common in the baseline model are not observed in the Oxford generated data. Except for a certain deviation of the generated data from the real data at the end of the charging process, the generated data often overlaps the real data in most regions of a single cycle. Visually, the generated data could not be distinguished even without applying filters. This conclusion is supported by the results from PDF and PCA. For both visualization tools, generated and real data distributions overlap each other. Such results definitely confirm the effectiveness of transfer learning: although the baseline dataset and the new dataset are unrelated at all, transfer learning allows us to utilize merely a small sample amount of the fresh data to train an effective model.

The setup of transfer learning for the NCA dataset is exactly the same as for the Oxford dataset. However, since the Oxford dataset is significantly different from the baseline dataset, while the NCA dataset demonstrates a similar trend to that of the baseline dataset, a reduced training duration is sufficient to achieve satisfactory results. Therefore, the baseline model needs to be trained on the NCA dataset with fewer (2000) epochs after transfer. Fig. 6 exhibits the excellent quality of generated data after such training. Notably, the generated data under a different conditional label (25 °C) is presented here. Its comparable excellent quality with the baseline model's generated data at 45 °C highlights the proposed framework's performance in treating each condition label consistently.

In addition to the quality of the generated data, the accuracy of the battery state estimator is a valuable measure of the performance of the proposed framework. In order to be closer to real-world usage scenarios, which typically involve small datasets, and to demonstrate that these will benefit more significantly from data augmentation techniques, battery state estimation will be performed based on the Oxford dataset and the NCA dataset. The GAN model after transfer learning will generate “pseudo-real data”, which can be used in different scenarios. Specifically, scenario 1 (data replacement) simulates a situation where part of the data is unavailable during the measurement process for various reasons. Half of the real measurement data will be replaced by generated data in this scenario. In scenario 2 (data enhancement), the situation of limited measurement resources is addressed. Here, an amount of generated data equal to the amount of real measurement data is added to the estimator training data set. As a result, the number of training data here is twice that of utilizing only real data.

Two state estimators are employed in this work: the Bi-GRU-based estimator and the transformer-based estimator. Their specific structures are detailed in Section 2.2.4. Different cells serve as the training and test sets of the state estimators. For a data-driven approach, this places a higher demand on the quality of the measurement data and thus can demonstrate the advantages of the proposed framework. The first 6 cells in the Oxford dataset are selected for the estimator's model training, and the last 2 are considered as the test set. The first 3 cells in the NCA dataset at 25 °C are utilized for the training set, and the fourth is applied for testing. Since discharge behavior is typically user-defined during battery usage, only the relatively more controllable charging data are used for the estimator training.

Fig. 7 illustrates SOH estimation accuracy for the Oxford test data set and for three different training data scenarios: real data, data replacement, and data enhancement. Clearly, both Bi-GRU-based and transformer-based estimators are consistently effective at tracking the battery's aging state throughout its lifespan. For this prolonged data series, the transformer provides a greater ability to obtain accurate information: it yields a lower average RMSE of those three usage scenarios. When performing data replacement, the estimation accuracy decreases slightly compared to employing only real data, but it still provides quite good accuracy. Considering that this scenario replaces half of the real data with generated data, this is equivalent to estimating SOH in the extreme case of 50% of measurements failing. Therefore, the achieved model quality under these rather challenging conditions can be considered exceptional. It seems likely that increased estimator accuracy is obtained when generated data replaces less real data. In the data enhancement scenario illustrated in Fig. 7(c and f), the SOH estimation accuracy gains further improvement, with an accuracy of around 0.7%, which is certainly satisfactory. Although the number of inputs in this scenario is twice as many compared to utilizing only real data, an increase in accuracy can be expected. However, the implementation of generated data for data augmentation does not bring additional resource consumption and excessive workload; this accuracy improvement seems “free” for the user. This also demonstrates the promising application of the proposed framework.

To comprehensively illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, we evaluated two extreme application scenarios in addition to the previously discussed scenarios: specifically, the estimation of SOH using solely 50% of either the real data or the generated data. The results of this evaluation are depicted in Fig. 8 . A notable conclusion from this analysis is that the reduced quantity of data does not fulfill the requirements for the Bi-GRU-based estimator. The model encounters a training failure, resulting in an R 2 value approaching 0, which indicates that the model contributes almost no valid information for SOH estimation under these circumstances. This observation further proves the proposed method's advantages: deep learning networks necessitate a high data volume, whereby an increased amount of data—regardless of its inherent noise levels—tends to enhance model training efficacy. Integrating generated data during training can effectively mitigate the risk of training failures. Conversely, while the Transformer model does not experience training failures with reduced datasets, utilizing only 50% of generated or actual data yields estimator accuracy that does not measure up to the data replacement scenario. This limitation becomes even more pronounced in the context of the other two application scenarios, which encompass higher data quality or larger data volumes. This conclusion further substantiates the efficacy of the proposed method.

Similarly, Fig. 9 displays the estimation accuracy of the NCA dataset for all usage scenarios. Generally, a transformer is considered the most efficient option for processing large data series. However, the Transformer-based approach provides lower accuracy with purely real data. This may be due to the NCA test set having a single cell, or the training data size being relatively small. The limited data amount means that the effect of outliers is exacerbated. Thus, at the end of the lifespan, the inaccuracy of SOH estimation affects overall accuracy more significantly. This shortcoming is compensated for by the application of data enhancement techniques, as presented in Fig. 9(f). The introduction of generated data makes outliers disappear, which emphasizes the advantages of the proposed framework. In addition to this, the proposed method exhibits a comparable trend as the Oxford dataset in two different scenarios: the data replacement scenario yields an acceptable slight decrease in accuracy and the data enhancement scenario improves the SOH estimation accuracy. The analysis includes scenarios utilizing either 50% real or 50% generated data, which are assessed separately within the NCA dataset. The findings are illustrated in Fig. 10 . Although there are no discernible indicators of training collapse, the range of SOH estimates exhibits a tendency to centralization when only 50% of the generated data is employed for both the Bi-GRU and Transformer models. This centralization indicates a model that can not effectively complete its learning process. While applying 50% real data yields marginally improved prediction accuracy compared to 50% generated data, this accuracy remains insufficient compared to any previously discussed scenarios. Thus, the effectiveness of the proposed method is reaffirmed in the context of the NCA dataset.

Traditional shallow networks have less stringent training data requirements compared to deep learning networks. To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed method for this category of neural networks, we also examined SOH estimators that utilize shallow networks alongside incremental capacity analysis (ICA). The findings demonstrate that the proposed method yields comparable benefits for this class of SOH estimators. A comprehensive analysis procedure is outlined in Appendix B.

Fig. 11 illustrates the SOC estimation results for the Oxford dataset in each scenario. Since the SOC estimation is based on the data processing method laid out in Section 3, sequence length as input is significantly reduced compared to the SOH estimation, because the SOC estimation inputs come from a sliding window sampling with a window size of 50 (Cf. Section 2.2.4.2). Bi-GRU and transformer exhibit in this case a diametrically opposite relationship of superiority and inferiority to that in SOH estimation with the Bi-GRU being considerably superior now. This also reflects that a more complex structure does not provide an advantage in the face of decreased sequence lengths. For the application of data-driven methods it is necessary to choose the model structure appropriate according to the data characteristics, especially the number of training data. In both usage scenarios, data replacement and data enhancement, the change in estimator accuracy exhibits a similar trend as for the SOH estimator: data replacement brings a slight decrease in accuracy, while data enhancement benefits the estimator’s model quality. However, it is worth noting that in the data replacement (data enhancement) scenario combined with the transformer, the RMSE is near 3% (1.8%). This is considered a low value for data in a laboratory measurement environment. However, the data replacement (data enhancement) scenario in combination with Bi-GRU obtains an RMSE of 1.01% (0.58%). By combining with a more appropriate model structure, the proposed framework can achieve the desired SOC estimation accuracy.

The conclusions drawn from the estimation of SOC using the NCA dataset are comparable to those obtained from the Oxford dataset (Fig. 12 ). However, the relatively shorter length of the inputs utilized for SOC estimation renders this data less compatible with Transformer-based approaches. The results of SOC estimation across various scenarios demonstrate a trend consistent with that observed in the Oxford dataset. In particular, when employing data replacement techniques on the NCA dataset, the accuracy of SOC estimation achieves an RMSE of 2.3%. This observation underscores the impact of input data characteristics on the performance of battery state estimators. Integrating an appropriate estimator architecture with effective data enhancement techniques can further enhance the accuracy of the battery state estimator.

The analysis of the SOC estimator also checks results derived from utilizing only 50% of either the real or generated data. These findings are depicted in Figs. 13 and 14
. The results obtained from both the Oxford dataset and the NCA dataset demonstrate a consistent trend: in contrast to the SOH estimation, both the Bi-GRU and Transformer models indicate that, although the accuracy of the SOC estimation based on 50% of the real data remains inferior to the scenario involving data replacement, the extent of this discrepancy is relatively limited. One plausible explanation for this phenomenon is that the length of the time series data employed as input for the SOC estimator is significantly shorter than that used for the SOH estimator. Accordingly, the volume of training data necessary for the model to effectively correlate the time series data with the output values is also considerably reduced. Consequently, even with only 50% of the available data, the SOC model exhibits reduced susceptibility to underfitting compared to the SOH model. In conclusion, while SOC estimation deep learning networks exhibit lesser demands regarding data volume, incorporating generated data continues to yield performance enhancements. This proposed method presents significant benefits for battery state estimators across various applications.

Accurate battery state estimation is essential for the efficient operation of battery systems. However, data-driven battery state estimation relies heavily on the quantity and quality of measurement data, which is frequently hampered by a shortage of experimental data. In this paper, we propose a solution framework by employing data augmentation techniques based on generative adversarial networks to enhance the quantity and quality of measurement data. This will provide researchers with limited measurement availability with a rich battery dataset and reduce the resources required to acquire the data. Considering the extremely high complexity and undesirable stability of traditional generative adversarial network training, a WGAN-GP structure is implemented in this work that solves the above-mentioned problems by combining the Wasserstein distance and the gradient penalty to optimize the loss function. In addition, the proposed C-DC-WGAN-GP structure applies the concept of conditional GAN and classical deep convolutional networks, which enables the proposed method to generate data for specific conditions. To further minimize the threshold for applying the proposed method, transfer learning is introduced to form the C-DC-WGAN-GP-TL framework, which allows the user to train a baseline model utilizing an easily accessible large dataset and then transfer the baseline model to a smaller dataset through transfer learning. The quality of the generated data is examined by means of visual comparisons, probability density functions, and principal component analysis. The results demonstrate that generated data cannot easily be distinguished from real data both for the baseline model and the model obtained by transfer learning.

In order to validate the impact of the proposed framework on battery state estimator accuracy, we evaluate SOH and SOC estimation accuracy for two different datasets after transfer learning through the Bi-GRU-based method and the transformer-based method, respectively. The above evaluations are performed for multiple scenarios, which represent employing purely real data, replacing half of the real data with generated data, and including the same amount of generated data as the number of real data to implement data enhancement. The results indicate that battery state estimator accuracy decreases only slightly in the data replacement scenario, while the estimator accuracy is further improved in the data enhancement scenario. Specifically, the SOH estimator can achieve up to 0.69% RMSE, while this value is 0.58% for the SOC estimator. The proposed framework achieves excellent performance on small datasets after transfer learning, providing a reliable method of enriching training data for battery state estimation. This can contribute to technological advancement in this field.

The proposed framework is a generalized framework capable of generating various types of time series data. Its applications are not limited to battery cycle data generation. For all kinds of data shortage problems, this framework can perform the task of data augmentation after adjusting the parameters. Its future implementations in different fields, such as abnormal state monitoring, aging trajectory prediction, etc., will be the focus of our next work.

Jinghua Sun: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Ankun Gu: Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of critical hyperparameters for GAN: Batch size and learning rate
ce:section.ce:para: Both batch size (BS) and learning rate (LR) are crucial hyperparameters that directly influence the effectiveness of model training and determine the performance of the proposed method. When selecting these two hyperparameters, a widely accepted empirical recommendation is that the batch size and learning rate should always be proportional to each other [51]. On the one hand, a smaller batch size introduces more noise into the learning process. At this point, a lower learning rate can lessen the impact of noise, thereby limiting training instability and divergence. On the other hand, using a larger batch size can enhance the accuracy of gradient estimation. This improvement allows the training process to better accommodate increases in the step size of the gradient updates, especially when a significant learning rate is employed.

Utilizing the aforementioned empirical recommendations as a foundation, we initially evaluated eight combinations of batch size and learning rate. Following the identification of the most appropriate parameter combination for this study, we conducted a further validation of this selection. This validation process involved two distinct phases: firstly, we fixed the learning rate from the selected combination and assessed the variations in training outcomes resulting from different batch sizes; secondly, we fixed the batch size from the combination and scrutinized the impact of various learning rates on the training results. This methodical approach to parameter selection provides a rationale for identifying the most optimal hyperparameter combination.
Fig. A.1
Evaluation of training outcomes with varied hyperparameters: (1) LR: 0.00005 & BS: 8; (2) LR: 0.0001 & BS: 16; (3) LR: 0.0002 & BS: 32; (4) LR: 0.0004 & BS: 64.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Evaluation of training outcomes with varied hyperparameters: (1) LR: 0.0008 & BS: 128; (2) LR: 0.0016 & BS: 256; (3) LR: 0.0032 & BS: 512; (4) LR: 0.0064 & BS: 1024.

ce:section.ce:para: Figs. A.1 and A.2 illustrate the training effects of eight different hyperparameter combinations, with four criteria introduced to weigh the comparison here: they are the direct comparison between real and generated data, PDF, PCA, and the discriminator loss. The overall pattern derived from these two diagrams indicates that smaller batch sizes and learning rates contribute significantly to enhancing the quality of generated data. This conclusion is supported by all four criteria involved in the diagrams. When the batch size is more significant than 128, there is a sharp drop in the quality of the generated data. Larger batch sizes can limit the diversity of gradient updates during training. Additionally, using higher learning rates may cause the model to overlook certain underlying data distributions, which hinders the formation of complex mappings between inputs and outputs.

Although small batch sizes and learning rates have been shown to be more optimal choices for data quality, the reduction of these two hyperparameters is not endless: too small values can lead to wasted computational resources and a lengthy training process. In order to reach an optimal balance between the quality of the generated data and the required computational resources, two metrics, FID (Fréchet Inception Distance) and training duration, are introduced into the evaluation of the hyperparameters. FID is a metric employed to assess the quality of GAN-generated data [52], which primarily measures the similarity between the distribution of generated samples and the distribution of real samples, thus comprehensively assessing the quality and diversity of generated samples.
(A.1)
