is the trace of a matrix. A lower FID value indicates that the distribution of the generated sample is more similar to the real sample and vice versa.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Evaluation of training duration and training quality across varying hyperparameter settings.

ce:section.ce:para:
Table A.1 demonstrates the FID values and training durations for the eight hyperparameter combinations described above. As can be seen from the table, the combination
LR = 0.0002 & B S = 32
can achieve the optimal balance between generation quality (FID value), training duration, and training stability: the FID value (4.17) is approaching the optimal, which implies high-quality generated samples, while the training time (14893.6 s), which is almost halved in comparison to smaller batch size, makes this set of hyperparameters impressively efficient. In addition, the discriminator loss when applying this set of hyperparameters demonstrates much more minimal fluctuations. This circumvents the problem of unstable training and also model converges to the local optimum due to significant discriminator loss.
Fig. A.3
Evaluation of training outcomes with a fixed learning rate of 0.0002: (1) BS: 8; (2) BS: 16; (3) BS: 32; (4) BS: 64.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Evaluation of training outcomes with a fixed learning rate of 0.0002: (1) BS: 128; (2) BS: 256; (3) BS: 512; (4) BS: 1024.
Evaluation of training outcomes with a batch size of 32: (1) LR: 0.02; (2) LR: 0.002; (3) LR: 0.0002; (4) LR: 0.00002.

ce:section.ce:para:
After choosing the optimal combination of
LR = 0.0002 & B S = 32
out of the eight combinations, the training results for the cases of fixing the learning rate or fixing the batch size, respectively, then varying the other hyperparameter, are exhibited in. Figs. A.3 through A.5 When the learning rate is fixed, as illustrated in Figs. A.3 and A.4, utilizing a minimal batch size, result in considerable fluctuations in the discriminator loss. While the training outcomes in this scenario may be commendable, such fluctuations render the training process susceptible to instability and the potential for training collapse. In contrast, substantial batch sizes (exceeding 64) result in a notable increase in the noise level of the generated data, thereby significantly compromising the quality of that data. When the batch size is constant (refer to Fig. A5), an excessively small learning rate renders the model highly vulnerable to becoming trapped in a local optimum, thereby compromising the quality and diversity of the generated data. Conversely, as the learning rate increases, the step size for parameter updates becomes excessively large. Consequently, the model fails to converge toward the optimal solution in a stable manner, resulting in oscillations around the vicinity of the optimal point. In conclusion, the parameter combination
LR = 0.0002 & B S = 32
is identified as the most appropriate for the dataset utilized in this study. However, when encountering a different dataset, re-evaluating and selecting new parameters may be necessary.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Evaluation of training duration and training quality with a fixed learning rate of 0.0002.
Evaluation of training duration and training quality with a fixed batch size of 32.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of proposed method on shallow network SOH estimator based on incremental capacity analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In contrast to deep learning techniques, such as those exemplified by Transformers, traditional shallow network-based approaches for SOH estimation do not impose significant requirements on the volume of data necessary for model training. Consequently, further evaluation of the proposed method's effectiveness concerning these estimation techniques is imperative.

A variant of the shallow forward propagation network (GA-BPNN, Genetic Algorithm Optimized Backpropagation Neural Network) proposed in our previous work will be used to form the SOH estimator [16]. At this stage, measurement data cannot be utilized as direct inputs for the estimator due to the inherent limitations of shallow networks in processing substantial volumes of time-series data. For this reason, the Incremental Capacity Analysis (ICA) technique is also invoked in this work. This method determines the incremental capacity value by applying a specific equation, thereby transforming the indistinct voltage plateaus observed on the voltage-charge (U-Q) curve into distinctly identifiable peaks on the incremental capacity (IC) curve.
(B.1)
IC = d Q / d U

The ICA technique exhibits an exceptional sensitivity to measurement noise [53]. To obtain smooth IC curves, this study utilizes the Lowess (locally weighted scatterplot smoothing) filtering technique on the measurement data about both voltage and charge. Subsequently, the IC values are calculated. Upon completion of the aforementioned operations, examples of the IC curves corresponding to the NCA dataset and the Oxford dataset are presented in Fig. B.1. As indicated in the figure, the peak heights and peak positions of the two IC peaks for the NCA cell, as well as one peak for the Oxford cell, will serve as inputs to the SOH estimator.
Fig. B.1
Examples of IC features utilized as inputs for the SOH estimator: NCA and Oxford datasets.

In the validation process of the shallow network SOH estimator utilizing ICA, the training and test sets for both datasets are partitioned in accordance with the methodology described in the main text. The accuracy of SOH estimation employing this method across various scenarios is illustrated in Fig. B.2, encompassing real data, data replacement, and data enhancement. Notably, the accuracy of the ICA-based SOH estimation consistently surpasses that of the deep learning approach relying directly on measurement data, as outlined in the main text. This discrepancy can be primarily attributed to the data preprocessing phase: the ICA-based methodology employs a filtering technique that effectively controls the noise level. Additionally, extracting IC features, including peak heights and positions, further refines the dataset. This will ensure that only those IC features that exhibit robust correlation with the SOH are retained within the model. Nevertheless, the limitations of this approach are apparent, particularly the stringent requirements imposed on the measurement environment, which may hinder its practical application in real-world settings. Consequently, it is prudent for BMS to concurrently consider both ICA-based methods and direct measurement data approaches to optimize the performance of SOH estimation under diverse operational conditions.
Fig. B.2
Accuracy of the ICA-based SOH estimator for NCA (a-c) and Oxford (d-f) test datasets: consider only experimental data as input, replace 50% experimental data with generated data, add an additional amount of generated data equal to the number of experimental data.

The overall impact of the proposed method on the shallow network SOH estimator across various scenarios exhibits a similarity to that observed in the deep learning network. In the NCA dataset, the data replacement scenario demonstrates a marginal yet acceptable decrease in accuracy, whereas the data enhancement scenario improves accuracy. This trend is particularly pronounced within the Oxford dataset. The accuracy achieved in the data replacement scenario is comparable to that obtained with real data, while the data enhancement scenario reveals a more significant increase in accuracy.

To effectively demonstrate the impact of the proposed method on the SOH estimator, we conducted an analysis using scenarios that utilized only 50% of both the real data and the generated data as inputs to the SOH estimator, respectively. The findings from these scenarios are illustrated in Fig. B.3. The validation of both datasets reveals a consistent trend: the accuracy of SOH estimation is significantly reduced when only 50% of the generated data is employed. In contrast, while the accuracy of the SOH estimation based on 50% of the actual data remains excellent, it is lower not only than that derived from the complete set of real data but also than the results obtained from the data replacement scenario. This suggests that, for the current standard method—which involves the shallow network SOH estimator based on ICA—the integration of generated data can enhance the accuracy of SOH estimation, regardless of the amount of data utilized. Furthermore, when considered alongside the results of the deep learning network validation presented in the main text, implementing the proposed method is demonstrated to be beneficial across a diverse range of SOH estimation techniques.
Fig. B.3
Accuracy of the ICA-based SOH estimator for NCA (a, b) and Oxford (c, d) test datasets: consider only 50% experimental or generated data as input.
